rationale_for_baseline_tier: The reaction is confined to the skin and bones (urticating lesions/flushing/bone pain) with no airway, circulatory or GI involvement, so it is not anaphylaxis and does not warrant emergency transport. It is managed with antihistamine up-dosing, cooling, NSAID avoidance and a very low-threshold anaphylaxis safety-net (T2 self-care + telehealth advice), with the in-date adrenaline autoinjector confirmed. Given recurrent-anaphylaxis history and living alone, the escalation threshold is very low. Any airway/breathing/circulatory/GI feature means adrenaline + 000 (T5). T2 is the minimum viable pass; a reflexive ambulance for an isolated flare is over-triage.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Throat/tongue swelling, voice change, wheeze, difficulty breathing, dizziness/faintness, or vomiting
escalation_edges.rationale: These indicate evolving anaphylaxis requiring adrenaline and emergency care.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If your throat/tongue swells, your voice changes, you wheeze or can't breathe properly, you feel faint, or you start vomiting — use your adrenaline pen and call 000 immediately.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: No in-date adrenaline available, or unsure how to use it
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Arrange prompt review to obtain/renew pens and an action plan
escalation_edges.rationale: An isolated mastocytosis patient must have emergency adrenaline cover.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If your adrenaline pens are missing or expired, arrange a prompt review to get replacements and an action plan.

contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Very low escalation threshold; ensure adrenaline access and advise checking in with someone.
Flags lifelong anaphylaxis risk and NSAID avoidance; needs prophylactic mast cell therapy and specialist review.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: This is a skin-and-bone mast cell flare, not a dangerous whole-body allergic reaction — your breathing, throat and blood pressure are unaffected. Up-dose your non-sedating antihistamine, add an H2 blocker, cool the skin, wear loose clothing, and avoid friction, heat and anti-inflammatory painkillers. Keep your adrenaline pens right beside you.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Self-care with telehealth/immunology follow-up; use adrenaline + 000 for the emergency features below.
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: Use adrenaline + 000 immediately for any airway/breathing/circulation/GI feature; otherwise manage at home.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Note the friction-triggered cutaneous/skeletal mast cell flare, negative anaphylaxis screen, and in-date adrenaline autoinjector.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Reassurance without an anaphylaxis screen/safety-net or adrenaline check, missing evolving anaphylaxis in an isolated recurrent-anaphylaxis patient.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: The reaction progresses to airway involvement and adrenaline is not used in time.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Ambulance/000 for an isolated cutaneous/skeletal flare with no systemic features, over-medicalising a manageable flare.
